Document [1] (Title: Periodontal Disease -- Staging -- Staging) Staging reflects the severity of periodontitis and the complexity of its management. This is determined by factors such as CAL, radiographic bone loss, and tooth loss, with the classification based on the site with the most severe bone loss. The stages include: Stage I—Initial periodontitis: Bone loss is limited to the coronal third (≤15%). Stage II—Moderate periodontitis: Bone loss extends beyond 15% but remains less than 30%. Stage III—Severe periodontitis with potential for additional tooth loss: Bone loss reaches the mid-third of the root (30%–60%). Stage IV—Severe periodontitis with potential for loss of dentition: Bone loss exceeds 60% and extends to the apical third.
Document [2] (Title: Cardiac Calcifications -- Continuing Education Activity) Cardiac calcifications carry different diagnostic and prognostic significance depending on their location. They can involve the coronary arteries, cardiac valves, myocardium, and pericardium. They are commonly observed on image modalities done for various reasons. To avoid the morbidity and mortality associated with cardiac calcifications (whether in the coronary arteries, cardiac valves, or myocardium/pericardium), they must be promptly diagnosed and treated. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of cardiac calcifications in various locations and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with these calcifications.

Document [6] (Title: Pericardial Calcification -- Treatment / Management) Diuretics have been used in the early stages of constrictive pericarditis to improve pulmonary and systemic congestion. These must be used cautiously because any decrease in intravascular volume may result in a corresponding reduction in cardiac output. [22] Surgical pericardiectomy is the gold standard for constrictive pericarditis and is potentially curative. [20] Pericardiectomy usually has a high success rate (70-80%) but also has a high perioperative mortality rate of 5% to 10%. Perioperative cardiovascular outcomes are usually influenced by New York Heart Association heart failure class, with NYHA Class I and II heart failure patients having better postoperative outcomes than NYHA Class III and IV heart failure. The presence and extent of pericardial calcification usually do not affect perioperative outcomes. [23]

Document [15] (Title: Calcified Plaque -- Treatment / Management) The treatment of calcified lesions during PCI is dependent on the severity of calcium. An OCT-based scoring system, known as the calcium-volume index or rule of 5's, helps to stratify the likelihood a calcified plaque would correlate with stent under-expansion if not treated with a lesion preparation strategy. Calcified lesions involving an arc of calcium more than 50% of the vessel (greater than 180 degrees), with a thickness exceeding 0.5mm and over 5 mm in length, are most strongly associated with stent under-expansion and should be treated with atherectomy when appropriate. [11] Calcified lesions with a mild to moderate degree of calcification can be treated with cutting, scoring, or sculpting balloons and often do not require atherectomy. Severely calcified lesions, however, are best managed with adjunctive atherectomy in advance of stent implantation. Not only does atherectomy improve the likelihood of successful stent delivery, but atherectomy modifies the plaque morphology of the lesion, facilitating adequate stent expansion by increasing the possibility of calcium fracture. [12] Maximizing stent expansion can help to reduce the likelihood of in-stent restenosis.

Document [17] (Title: Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease -- Staging) Multiple classifications can be utilized to describe Legg-Calve-Perthes disease. The lateral pillar, or Herring, classification is widely accepted with the best interobserver agreement. It is generally determined at the beginning of the fragmentation stage, approximately 6 months after initial symptom presentation. It cannot be used accurately if the patient has not entered the fragmentation stage radiographically. The goal is to provide prognostic information. This classification is based on the height of the lateral pillar on the AP X-ray image. Group A: The lateral pillar is at full height with no density changes. This group has a consistently good prognosis. Group B: The lateral pillar maintains greater than 50% height. There will be a poor outcome if the bone age is greater than 6. Group C: Less than 50% of the lateral pillar height is maintained. All patients will experience a poor outcome. [1]

Document [20] (Title: Orbital Atherectomy -- Technique or Treatment) Advance the crown to begin atherectomy via the advancer knob no faster than 1 mm per second. Once the crown is orbiting, maintaining continuous movement is important as remaining in one location can cause damage to the coronary vessel. Under fluoroscopy, ensure the crown and crown advancer knob move in a 1 to 1 fashion. Perform a series of intermittent treatment intervals back and forth across the lesion, followed subsequently by a rest period with the crown on the proximal side of the lesion.

Document [24] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation) Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficult if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the free gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (“heatless stone”) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.

All of these CT techniques serve as the primary method for detecting mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية). Through CT imaging, clinicians can identify mediastinal masses located in distinct compartments—mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, and superior‑mediastinal regions—and can also recognize masses that contain calcium. Thus, chest CT, in its various contrast‑based and non‑contrast forms, is the essential diagnostic tool for characterizing the presence, location, and composition of mediastinal masses, informing subsequent clinical management and patient care.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622) CT angiography of thorax with contrast is classified as a chest CT scan, representing one specific type of computed tomography performed on the thorax using intravenous contrast to visualize vascular structures. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax without contrast material is also a chest CT scan, described as a narrower subcategory that acquires images without the use of contrast agents. A further refined variant, diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, is likewise a narrower subcategory of chest CT, combining an initial non‑contrast acquisition with subsequent contrast‑enhanced imaging and extended coverage. In clinical practice, chest CT scans serve as the imaging method for evaluating mediastinal masses that contain calcium, with computed tomography of the chest being the specific finding method used to detect and characterize such calcified mediastinal lesions. Together, these relationships illustrate the hierarchical organization of thoracic CT techniques and their role in diagnosing calcium‑bearing mediastinal pathology.
